name: Keres
description: The Keres (singular: Ker) are female death-spirits in Greek mythology, often depicted as winged demons who haunted battlefields, thirsting for the blood of the slain. Unlike the personified Thanatos (death), the Keres represent violent, gruesome death, particularly in battle or through accident. They were daughters of Nyx (Night) and are associated with doom, fate, and the inevitable end. These dark goddesses actively sought out those nearing their demise, swooping down to claim their souls and drag them to the Underworld. Their appearance was often terrifying, with sharp claws, gnashing teeth, and a hunger that could never be sated. While some sources portray them as messengers of fate, delivering death as ordained, others emphasize their predatory nature, reveling in the chaos and suffering of mortals. They differ from the Fates (Moirai) who determine the course of life; the Keres merely enact the moment of death. Their presence instilled fear and dread, a constant reminder of the fragility of life and the omnipresence of death, especially in times of war. The Keres highlight the stark and often brutal realities of mortality in ancient Greek thought, presenting death not as a peaceful transition, but as a violent abduction. They are frequently depicted in artwork associated with warfare, such as on shields or vases, symbolizing the horrors of combat and the ever-present threat of a gruesome end. The Keres serve as a chilling personification of the grim reaper, embodying the unavoidable fate that awaits all mortals.
subtitle: Spirits of Violent Death
appearance: The Keres are typically depicted as winged female spirits, often with sharp claws, gnashing teeth, and a terrifying visage. Their clothing, if any, is ragged and bloodstained. Some accounts describe them as having dark, shadowy forms, while others portray them with more grotesque, demonic features. Their eyes are often described as burning with a malevolent hunger.
habitat: Battlefields, locations of violent death, the Underworld
behavior: The Keres are driven by an insatiable hunger for blood and souls. They actively seek out dying individuals, particularly those slain in battle, and eagerly drag them to the Underworld. They revel in chaos and suffering, embodying the most violent and gruesome aspects of death. They are merciless and uncaring, driven only by their primal instincts.
weaknesses: Certain Offerings to appease related deities (e.g., Nyx)

keyMyths.title: The Shield of Achilles
keyMyths.description: Homer's Iliad depicts Keres as figures on Achilles' shield, reveling in the carnage of battle and dragging away the slain. This portrayal emphasizes their association with war and violent death.

keyMyths.title: Hesiod's Theogony
keyMyths.description: Hesiod names the Keres as daughters of Nyx, further solidifying their connection to darkness and death. This lineage establishes them as primordial forces of the night.

sources.author: Homer
sources.description: Epic poem depicting the Trojan War, featuring the Keres on Achilles' shield.
sources.text: Epic poem depicting the Trojan War, featuring the Keres on Achilles' shield.

sources.author: Hesiod
sources.description: Genealogical account of the Greek gods, listing the Keres as daughters of Nyx.
